Note zu RQ und Hypos

Es sollte Möglich sein, die jeweiligen Modell in einer oder beiden Hypothesen zu verordnen. Sprich: ich untersuche mit meinem Modell H1 und finde dass mehr reviews den Preis steigen lassen blablabla. Zudem kann es in gewissen Modellen sinn machen, beide Hypothesen anzuschauen und zu beantworten. es kann aber auch sein, dass man zb H1 anschaut und mit Faktoren wie “accommodates” kontrolliert. dann habe ich zwar location-sided-Indicators, jedoch habe ich keinen Fokus auf die H2.**

Eine dritte Option ist, dass ich nicht eine der beiden Hypothesen untersuche, sondern die zweite Forshcungsfrage und versuche vorhersagen zu treffen. dies macht beispielsweise beim SVM sinn. da müsst ihr selbst enscheiden, welchen Fokus ihr setzen wollt

Coding Conventions. • data sets are named with the prefix “d.” (e.g., d.cats). • linear model objects are named with the prefix “lm.” (e.g., lm.cats) • plots are named with the prefix “p.”

• within the text R-objects are written using code formatting (e.g., lm.cats)

• variable names are written using italic (e.g., Body.weight) • these conventions are arbitrary, but hopefully useful to the reader

1 Introduction

1.1 Business Case

We propose a data-driven pricing decision-support system for Airbnb hosts in the Canton of Vaud, providing market-aligned price recommendations based on listing characteristics. The tool targets both new and existing hosts, using inputs such as location, room type, guest capacity, availability, ratings, and superhost status. Six machine learning models, LM, GLM Poisson, GLM Binomial, GAM, SVM and a Neural Network, are applied to identify key price drivers and generate accurate predictions. The objective is to balance revenue and occupancy, helping hosts understand their market value. A key feature is interpretability, enabling users to see why a certain price is recommended. From a business perspective, the system aims to increase booking rates, reduce price dispersion, and improve host satisfaction. The model is tailored to the Canton of Vaud to capture regional economic effects.

1.2 Research Questions

Several questions can be derived from the business case. These questions are intended to help us examine pricing dynamics and facilitate the development of a pricing strategy for Airbnb.

  • RQ1 - First, we need to determine which factors influence guests’ willingness to pay. Furthermore, the nature of this influence is, of course, central to our analysis.
  • RQ2 - Once these factors are identified, a second question immediately arises: to what extent can these factors be used to predict whether the prices of a rental property are justified?

Willingness to pay is actually the central variable here. However, it is not easy to measure, which is why a different approach must be taken. The approach we have chosen stems from the idea that the market generally functions well and that landlords intuitively already align with willingness to pay. As a result, the existing price for a rental property can also be assumed to be the price that tenants are willing to pay. Any deviations are offset in our subsequent calculations by the large number of observations. Thus, on average, the actual price will correspond to the price that tenants are willing to pay.

1.3 Hypothesis

  • H1: When host-centered indicators improve, tenants’ willingness to pay increases.
  • H2: When location-centered indicators improve, tenants’ willingness to pay increases.

Host-centered indicators are those that relate to the host’s performance, such as ratings or Superhost status. In contrast, location-centered indicators are those that relate to factors such as the size of the rental property.

2 Data Preparation

First step: Loading Packages

Second step: Reading all available Datasets

Cleaning the Airbnb-Listings Data. We keep only the variables needed for our project. We reduce the amount of variables to exact 20, according to the requirements by our lecturers.

According to the requirements for this paper, the dataset is limited to a maximum of 20 variables. To meet this specification, we have removed columns from the dataset that will not be used in our analysis. Consequently, d.listings_clean is our final, cleaned dataset, which we will use to run all subsequent models.

'data.frame':   3876 obs. of  20 variables:
 $ host_response_time          : chr  "within an hour" "N/A" "within a few hours" "N/A" ...
 $ host_response_rate          : num  100 NA 100 NA 100 100 100 100 100 100 ...
 $ host_acceptance_rate        : num  100 NA 79 100 100 100 94 91 96 62 ...
 $ host_is_superhost           : logi  FALSE FALSE TRUE FALSE TRUE TRUE ...
 $ host_verifications          : chr  "['email', 'phone']" "['email', 'phone']" "['email', 'phone']" "['email', 'phone']" ...
 $ host_has_profile_pic        : chr  "t" "t" "t" "t" ...
 $ host_identity_verified      : chr  "t" "t" "t" "t" ...
 $ neighbourhood_group_cleansed: chr  "Aigle" "Nyon" "Aigle" "Lausanne" ...
 $ latitude                    : num  46.4 46.5 46.3 46.5 46.5 ...
 $ longitude                   : num  7.13 6.33 7.06 6.64 6.8 ...
 $ property_type               : Factor w/ 63 levels "Barn","Boat",..: 18 44 18 16 52 18 18 44 11 18 ...
 $ room_type                   : Factor w/ 4 levels "Entire home/apt",..: 1 3 1 1 2 1 1 3 1 1 ...
 $ accommodates                : int  3 5 6 3 4 2 2 1 3 14 ...
 $ bathrooms                   : num  1 NA 3 1.5 1 1 1 1 1 2.5 ...
 $ beds                        : int  2 4 4 1 3 2 1 1 2 10 ...
 $ price                       : num  107 725 590 144 254 277 101 34 127 278 ...
 $ minimum_nights              : int  3 4 4 3 1 1 5 2 2 2 ...
 $ number_of_reviews           : int  70 0 18 22 85 125 26 292 41 197 ...
 $ estimated_occupancy_l365d   : int  42 0 0 0 42 120 0 78 150 78 ...
 $ review_scores_rating        : num  4.74 NA 4.94 4.68 4.75 4.87 4.23 4.67 4.95 4.68 ...

Subsequently, we calculated several additional features to include them as permanent columns in the dataset, ensuring they do not have to be recomputed for each analysis. These include: the log-transformed price—as price data is typically heavily right-skewed—the price per guest (total price divided by the number of guests accommodated), a boolean indicator for high demand, and a binary variable identifying whether the price exceeds the median.

d.airbnb_listings_feat <- d.listings_clean %>%
  mutate(
    log_price = log(price),
    price_per_guest = price / pmax(accommodates, 1),
    high_demand = ifelse(estimated_occupancy_l365d > 200, 1, 0),
    price_high = as.integer(price > median(price, na.rm =TRUE))
  )

3 Understanding the Data

To inform our modelling choices, we briefly walk through how the data is structured e.g. with distributions, variable types, and edge cases. Airbnb already knows this data well, so we keep the commentary short and focus on what actually matters for the models that follow.

During our exploratory data analysis, we identified one ‘Private room’ listing with an unrealistically high price of 2’147’483’647 CHF. We excluded this single observation from our dataset as it is an extreme outlier that does not reflect the true market conditions and would negatively skew our predictive models.

in addition to the one outlier we wanted to remove one NA specifically

3.1 Prices

Prices are heavily right-skewed. After log-transformation the distribution is much closer to normal. That is the form we use for the LM, GAM and most diagnostics later.

3.2 Capacity & Rating

Most listings are built for 2 or 4 guests. Review scores cluster heavily between 4 and 5. Both hosts and guests rate each other, which probably produces a leniency bias on top of any genuine quality signal.

3.3 Room Types & Superhost Status

Entire homes/apartments and Private rooms dominate the inventory; Hotel rooms and Shared rooms are marginal. Roughly 30% of hosts are Superhosts. This is a meaningful minority, but far from the norm.

3.4 Log Prices and rooms / neighbourhoods

Among room types, hotel rooms post the highest median with a tight distribution. Likely a sign of more professional pricing. Entire homes and private rooms show the same shape but a long upper tail (luxury villas, chalets). Shared rooms are the cheapest option as expected. Across neighbourhoods, Riviera-Pays-d’Enhaut and Aigle sit at the top, Gros-de-Vaud at the bottom. Almost all top neighbourhoods show high-end outliers, confirming that luxury listings are spread across the canton, though the baseline price (the “floor”) varies significantly by location.

3.5 Host Variables

Hosts in Vaud are highly responsive and reliable: most reply within an hour, response and acceptance rates pile up at 100%, and nearly everyone has a profile picture and verified identity. The downside for modelling: these variables show very little variation, so they probably won’t carry much weight as predictors.

3.6 Properties & Neighborhoods

Entire rental units dominate the property mix, followed by private rooms in rental units. Specialty types like chalets and villas are present but rare. Geographically, Aigle and Lausanne are the hubs — Aigle alone has over 1,000 listings. In contrast, regions like Gros-de-Vaud show significantly less activity in the top 10 ranking.

3.7 Furnishing

Bathroom and bed counts cluster at the low end as expected. The bed distribution has a few extreme outliers (40+ beds), which could be large hostels or dormitories. We checked a handful manually but didn’t verify each one, so they remain in the data as potential noise.

3.8 Utilisation

The histograms illustrate the distributions of booking requirements, guest engagement, and listing utilization.

Minimum nights (log scale) shows a clear preference for short stays with a 1 to 3 nights dominance. A handful of listings enforce 365-day minimums, which is why we plot on a log scale. Review counts are L-shaped: many listings have very few reviews, a small group has hundreds. Estimated occupancy follows the same pattern, with a small spike at the very top end — those are likely commercial operators running close to full capacity year-round.

3.9 Feature Engineered Data

High demand is heavily imbalanced: very few listings cross our threshold. Accuracy would be a misleading metric for this target, so for classification on it we’d lean on F1 and precision-recall instead. High price is balanced by design — we split at the median, so both classes have equal weight. The GLM Binomial uses this target.

3.10 Verifications

Most hosts verify themselves with email plus phone — that’s the platform baseline. A smaller group adds a work email, which often signals professional or commercial hosts. Profiles with one or zero verifications are very rare.

4 Data Quality

We checked the dataset for missing values using the naniar and visdat packages. Using vis_dat() and gg_miss_var(), we identified that the missing data is not uniformly distributed but concentrated in specific features. The plots below show where NAs sit and how they overlap. The biggest blocks of missing data are in the rate columns (response, acceptance) and the review score variables. For the rates, we considered treating NAs as zero but decided against it — that would penalise hosts who simply have no reported value rather than a low one. For review scores, the data is most likely Missing Not At Random: a listing without reviews is usually a new or unbooked listing rather than a poorly-rated one. Imputing zeros would mislabel these as bad listings, and imputing the mean would artificially shrink variance. We therefore drop these observations from the models that need a rating, rather than imputing.

4.1 Missing Value Patterns

The plot below summarises the share of missing values per variable. Anything above ~10% gets explicit handling further down the pipeline.

4.2 NA Check on Key Variables

A quick count of NAs on the variables we use most often in the models below. lm() and svm() silently drop rows with missing values, which can quietly shrink the training set — this check makes sure we know exactly how much.

Count of Missing Values per Variable
Missing_Values
price 0
log_price 0
accommodates 0
number_of_reviews 0
host_is_superhost 0

5 Exploratory Data Analysis

5.1 Overall Market Overview (Prices & Capacity)

This is the absolute minimum, maximum, median, and mean for the most important numeric variables.

     price          log_price      accommodates    number_of_reviews
 Min.   :  20.0   Min.   :2.996   Min.   : 1.000   Min.   :  0.00   
 1st Qu.:  80.0   1st Qu.:4.382   1st Qu.: 2.000   1st Qu.:  1.00   
 Median : 120.0   Median :4.787   Median : 3.000   Median :  7.00   
 Mean   : 178.7   Mean   :4.858   Mean   : 3.755   Mean   : 29.53   
 3rd Qu.: 185.0   3rd Qu.:5.220   3rd Qu.: 5.000   3rd Qu.: 32.00   
 Max.   :9342.0   Max.   :9.142   Max.   :16.000   Max.   :637.00

The mean price sits well above the median, which means a small number of luxury listings is pulling the average upward. The price range stretches over several orders of magnitude. For any market-level summary we use the median to avoid this skew.

5.2 Analysis by Room Type

Since we want to know how many different types of rooms there are, we group the data and calculate the counts, percentages, and price ranges for each type.

# A tibble: 4 × 7
  room_type       Total_Listings Percentage_of_Market Min_Price Median_Price
  <fct>                    <int>                <dbl>     <dbl>        <dbl>
1 Entire home/apt           2907                 75          24        141  
2 Private room               940                 24.3        20         67.5
3 Shared room                 19                  0.5        23         47  
4 Hotel room                   8                  0.2        54        264. 
# ℹ 2 more variables: Average_Price <dbl>, Max_Price <dbl>

Entire homes and apartments make up roughly three quarters of the market. Hotel rooms have the highest median at 264.5 CHF, shared rooms the lowest at 47 CHF. The same pattern we already saw in the log-price boxplots above.

5.3 Detailed property_type Analysis

We already analyzed room_type, but Airbnb often has dozens of property_types (e.g., “Entire chalet”, “Private room in castle”…). Summarizing the Top 10 property types in the Canton of Vaud provides us with a great market insight.

Top 10 Property Types in the Canton of Vaud
property_type Total_Listings Median_Price
Entire rental unit 1973 128.0
Private room in rental unit 409 64.0
Entire home 287 250.0
Entire chalet 219 250.0
Private room in bed and breakfast 194 69.5
Entire condo 184 136.5
Private room in home 154 66.0
Entire villa 50 370.0
Private room in villa 45 68.0
Room in hotel 40 177.0

Standard rental units dominate, but specialty properties like chalets, villas, homes command a noticeable premium. The LM and GAM both include room_type for exactly this reason.

5.4 Correlation Matrix (Crucial before Machine Learning)

Before we train our LM, GAM, or SVM, we need to know how our numerical variables relate to each other. This helps us identify multicollinearity (when two predictors provide the same information) and strong price drivers.

Correlation Matrix of Key Numerical Drivers
log_price accommodates number_of_reviews
log_price 1.00 0.65 -0.16
accommodates 0.65 1.00 -0.04
number_of_reviews -0.16 -0.04 1.00

The heatmap confirms what we already suspected: accommodates, beds and bathrooms correlate strongly with each other. A strong sign of multicollinearity to watch for in the LM (see LM Chapter). price and log_price track capacity as expected; review variables form their own cluster. number_of_reviews and estimated_occupancy_l365d move together, because listings that get booked more naturally collect more reviews.

5.5 Log Price vs. Accommodates

The most important Relationship in the dataset from what we see in the correlation matrix above.

Capacity is the strongest single predictor in the dataset, and the relationship looks roughly linear on the log scale, which is exactly why we work with log(price) throughout the LM and GAM.

5.6 Log Price by Review Score Group

We display review_scores_rating as a histogram, but never in relation to the price.

Medians across rating groups are surprisingly close. Higher-rated listings do not automatically charge more. A first hint at the Superhost pattern we’ll come back to: in this market, reputation correlates with occupancy strategies more than with price premiums.

5.7 Spatial Data: Mapping the Listings by Log(Price)

Hot spots cluster along Lake Geneva and around Lausanne, with rural and alpine areas trailing off. This is exactly the surface the GAM picks up later with its 2D spline — and the reason we use raw coordinates rather than a postcode dummy.

5.8 Price per Guest Distribution

We calculated price per guest as a feature then visualized them.

Most listings sit between 30 and 100 CHF per guest. Hotel rooms charge the highest per-head price; shared rooms are clearly the cheapest. Entire homes look expensive overall but actually offer the best value once you split the cost across guests.

5.9 Superhost vs. Regular Host Breakdown

This business case specifically mentions superhost status. So we took a look to see how many there are and if they actually charge more or even less.

# A tibble: 2 × 5
  host_is_superhost Total_Hosts Percentage Median_Price Avg_Reviews
  <chr>                   <int>      <dbl>        <dbl>       <dbl>
1 No                       2805       72.4          122        17.3
2 Yes                      1069       27.6          112        61.6

At first glance the boxplot shows almost no difference in median price between Superhosts and regular hosts. This is our first sign that the Superhost badge does not translate into a price premium — a pattern that the GLM Binomial, the GAM and the Neural Network will all confirm independently in the next chapters.

6 Modelling Approach

We build six models, each looking at price or its proxy, occupancy from a different angle. The point isn’t to pick one winner. It’s to triangulate. When models with very different mechanics converge on the same answer, we can trust it more than any single result.

6.1 Models and their roles

Model Question Hypothesis Output
Linear Model Which variables shift price linearly? H1 Coefficients
GLM Poisson Which variables drive occupancy? H1 + H2 Rate ratios
GLM Binomial Who ends up in the upper price half? H1 Odds ratios
GAM What does the non-linear price surface look like? H2 (with H1 as control) Smooth curves + fair price per listing
SVM Is a given listing under-, fair-, or overpriced? RQ2 (prediction) Classification verdict
ANN Does any host effect survive flexible controls? H1 Predicted prices for hypothetical hosts

6.2 How the models connect

The LM is our baseline — anything more complex needs to clearly beat it. The GLM Poisson runs the same question on a different target (occupancy instead of price), which lets us spot situations where a variable affects one but not the other. The GLM Binomial reduces the price question to a yes/no decision (above or below median), which makes the host effect cleaner to read.

The GAM is the strongest single model in this study. It captures the non-linear effects we already saw in the EDA and adds a 2D spatial term that no other model has. The SVM then operationalises the GAM into a working tool — given a listing and a chosen price, it returns Underpriced, Fair, or Overpriced.

The ANN doesn’t add new information so much as it checks our assumptions. If a flexible neural network barely beats the GAM, the GAM is already capturing most of the available signal — and we can trust the interpretable model over the black box.

6.3 Train/Test discipline

The GAM, SVM and ANN are evaluated on held-out test data. The split is made once — on the cleaned dataset, before any model fits — and reused throughout. This matters especially for the SVM: its training labels come from the GAM’s fair-price band, so if we split after labelling, GAM information would leak into the SVM’s test set. We split first, fit the GAM only on the training set, then derive labels.

7 Linear Model (TIMON)

7.1 Theory

With this initial model, we aim to examine the first hypothesis if host-centered indicators shift price?

For this, we use the review_scores_rating. We also include the factor host_is_superhost to distinguish between the two groups. We measure willingness to pay here using the effective price of the rental property. Additionally, we introduce two control variables. First, the size of the rental property, as this obviously influences the rental price, and second, the type of room. This also significantly influences the price.

A linear model allows us to analyze multiple variables and their effect on a dependent variable. It is important that the dependent variable be normally distributed. Since this is not the case here, as is often the case with price variables, this variable is logarithmized. We assumed that there is a risk of high collinearity in our data. Therefore we checked for it, to make sure, that the results are robust.

7.2 Model

7.3 Visualisation

7.4 Interpretation

The model shows that both the hosts’ rating and their Superhost status have a significant influence. The rating has a positive effect of 0.071. In contrast, Superhost status has a negative effect of -0.114. The assumption that tenants are willing to pay more when renting from a Superhost therefore does not necessarily seem to hold true. One possible explanation for this phenomenon is that Superhosts may have a better understanding of the market because they have been active for a longer time, and therefore set lower prices in order to achieve a higher occupancy rate. This is relevant for Airbnb, as these results show that reviews on the host side are a key factor in price setting. It is also interesting that Superhosts might develop their own understanding for how prices can be set. The exact reasons underlying this difference cannot be examined within the scope of this paper. Through this analysis we can also answer the first hypothesis. Host centered Variables, in this case the review_scores_rating and host_is_superhost, do change the price of a venue.

7.5 Limitations

A linear model often comes with certain limitations, as the degree of complexity is limited. We can only observe linear effects. We can assume that capacity is not a linear effect, since the price of a location can vary depending on whether it accommodates 3 or 4 people, whereas the effect of one fewer person is less significant if the location accommodates 15 or 16 people. A GAM model could reveal such effects. Another limitation is the absence of the “location” variable. While we could include latitude and longitude, these variables alone do not have a significant impact on price. For example, we would need the distance to the nearest city center to measure this accurately.

8 GLM Poisson (PASCAL)

8.1 Theory

With the GLM model with Poisson-family / quasi-poisson family is used to analyse count data. Count data is strictly positive and counted (not continuous). Using this model we want to see which variables lead to the highest occupancy, meaning most of the time the airbnb is rented out. As the data is probably overdispersed, meaning the data has not the same variance and much more cheap listings than expensive ones, we need to use a quasi-poisson model that can handle this kind of data much better. First we fit a normal poisson-family, then check for overdispersion, and if necessary we use a quasi-poisson-family.

8.2 Link to Hypothesis

DAVIDE Vorschlag: This is the only model in our set that targets occupancy rather than price. Both hypotheses are in scope — host-centered indicators (H1) and structural indicators (H2) can both drive how often a listing gets booked. The answer turns out to be the missing piece of the Superhost puzzle the other models will surface.

8.3 Model

Comparing Models (Dispersion Ratio: 56.53)
Model term estimate std.error statistic p.value
Poisson (Intercept) 2.803 0.043 64.817 0.000
price -0.003 0.000 -81.858 0.000
host_is_superhostTRUE 0.957 0.005 189.086 0.000
review_scores_rating 0.226 0.009 24.766 0.000
accommodates 0.038 0.001 26.763 0.000
room_typeHotel room 0.553 0.038 14.504 0.000
room_typePrivate room -0.136 0.006 -21.649 0.000
room_typeShared room -0.188 0.035 -5.338 0.000
Quasi-Poisson (Intercept) 2.803 0.367 7.642 0.000
price -0.003 0.000 -9.652 0.000
host_is_superhostTRUE 0.957 0.043 22.294 0.000
review_scores_rating 0.226 0.078 2.920 0.004
accommodates 0.038 0.012 3.156 0.002
room_typeHotel room 0.553 0.323 1.710 0.087
room_typePrivate room -0.136 0.053 -2.553 0.011
room_typeShared room -0.188 0.299 -0.629 0.529

8.4 Crossvalidation

8.5 Interpretations

8.5.1 Interpretation GLM-Poisson

As expected, the GLM with a Poisson distribution produced overdispersion, since the variability in the Airbnb data was to be expected. The model calculated the standard errors too small, which is why all variables were highly significant in the first output. The dispersion was then calculated and found to be greater than 1, specifically 56.53. A quasi-Poisson model was used as a solution, which adjusts the standard errors upward.

8.5.2 Interpretation GLM-Quasipoisson

The GLM with quasi-Poisson distribution has corrected the overdispersion. The standard errors were adjusted upwards (significantly) and now provide a realistic picture of which factors truly have an influence on occupancy. Interpretations: - Price -> highly significant negative. As expected: The higher the price, the lower the occupancy - Superhost: highly significant positive: Attention, logged rates, therefore calculate the effect with the exponential function exp(0.9577) = 2.6. Being a Superhost increases occupancy by 2.6 times compared to a normal host. - Reviews: Positive and significant. Better reviews lead to more bookings. - Capacity: Positive and significant. The more people that fit, the more bookings. - Room types: In the intercept is “Entire home/apt”. Private Room is booked significantly worse than the entire accommodation. Shared Room has a high p-value. Statistically, it cannot be said whether the occupancy of a shared room differs from the entire apartment.

8.5.3 Interpretation Cross-validation

To ensure that the higher number of variables does not cause overfitting, we use cross-validation. In this process, we repeatedly split the data into new subsets, which we use to retrain and re-evaluate the model. We then check to see if there are any significant fluctuations. If the model were overfitting, we would repeatedly observe random fluctuations. In our case, however, we only get a single outlier, which suggests that we have a stable model without overfitting.

8.6 Business Case (Cmmt Davide: ich würde löschen)

The glm.quasipoisson model directly supports our revenue management objectives by identifying the core demand-side drivers of estimated_occupancy_l365d. It strongly supports H1 by demonstrating that host-centered indicators drastically increase demand; for example, achieving host_is_superhost status boosts expected occupancy by approximately 2.6 times. The model also confirms H2, showing that physical attributes like larger accommodates capacities and renting an entire home over a Private room significantly improve booking rates. These findings provide hosts with actionable, non-pricing levers to optimize their overall yield. Ultimately, improving review_scores_rating or attaining Superhost status offers hosts a powerful “occupancy buffer,” allowing them to command higher prices while maintaining strong booking volumes.

8.7 Limitations

A primary limitation of this model is the potential for reverse causality, as higher occupancy naturally generates more reviews, making it easier to maintain high ratings and Superhost status. Additionally, the deletion of 714 observations due to missingness could introduce survivorship bias if these removed data points represent poorly managed or unsuccessful listings. Furthermore, our feature set omits critical demand drivers like precise neighborhood desirability, seasonality, and listing photo quality, meaning unobserved factors likely account for significant residual variance. Because the Quasi-Poisson approach uses a quasi-likelihood function, it does not produce an AIC value (shown as AIC: NA), which complicates direct statistical comparisons with non-quasi models. A potential next step could be exploring a Negative Binomial model as an alternative method to handle overdispersion while retaining standard likelihood metrics for easier comparison.

9 GLM Binomial (DAVIDE)

9.1 Theory

When the outcome we want to model is binary — yes/no, above/below — a standard linear regression no longer fits. A Binomial GLM with a logit link is the natural choice: it bounds predictions between 0 and 1 and returns coefficients we can read as log-odds. Exponentiating gives odds ratios, which are easier to communicate to a non-technical audience. Our binary target price_high equals 1 if a listing’s price is above the median and 0 otherwise. The median split gives us two equally sized classes, so the model is not biased toward a majority outcome.

9.2 Link to Hypothesis

This model speaks mainly to H1: do host-centered indicators (Superhost status, ratings) push up the price? The control variables (accommodates, room_type) are not the focus but make sure we don’t confuse host effects with listing characteristics.

9.3 Model

Feature Engeneering


Call:
glm(formula = price_high ~ host_is_superhost + accommodates + 
    room_type + review_scores_rating, family = binomial(link = "logit"), 
    data = d.airbnb_listings_feat)

Coefficients:
                      Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    
(Intercept)           -4.16296    0.62153  -6.698 2.11e-11 ***
host_is_superhostTRUE -0.36687    0.09900  -3.706 0.000211 ***
accommodates           0.60632    0.02954  20.528  < 2e-16 ***
room_typeHotel room    1.75114    0.84063   2.083 0.037240 *  
room_typePrivate room -1.53809    0.14060 -10.939  < 2e-16 ***
room_typeShared room  -3.48212    1.46802  -2.372 0.017693 *  
review_scores_rating   0.48314    0.13097   3.689 0.000225 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

(Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)

    Null deviance: 4376.1  on 3159  degrees of freedom
Residual deviance: 3046.9  on 3153  degrees of freedom
  (714 Beobachtungen als fehlend gelöscht)
AIC: 3060.9

Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 5
GLM Binomial: Odds Ratios with 95% Confidence Intervals
term estimate odds_ratio or_lower or_upper p.value
(Intercept) -4.1629586 0.016 0.004 0.051 0.0000
host_is_superhostTRUE -0.3668731 0.693 0.570 0.841 0.0002
accommodates 0.6063184 1.834 1.732 1.945 0.0000
room_typeHotel room 1.7511366 5.761 1.259 40.642 0.0372
room_typePrivate room -1.5380852 0.215 0.162 0.281 0.0000
room_typeShared room -3.4821194 0.031 0.002 0.334 0.0177
review_scores_rating 0.4831448 1.621 1.262 2.109 0.0002

9.4 Visualisation

A coefficient table is hard to read for hosts. The plot below shows the predicted probability of charging an above-median price across listing sizes, split by Superhost status. Everything else is held at its average so the two lines differ only in the variable we care about.

9.5 Interpretation GLM Binomial

After controlling for size, room type and rating, Superhosts have 31% lower odds of charging an above-median price (OR = 0.69, CI [0.57, 0.84], p = 0.0002).

The control variables behave as expected: each additional guest nearly doubles the odds of being in the upper price half (OR = 1.83), private and shared rooms are far less likely to land above the median, and an one-point increase in rating raises the odds by about 62%. The hotel-room coefficient is significant but its wide confidence interval (1.26 to 40.6) reflects how few hotel listings are in the dataset.

This is the opposite of what H1 would predict. Superhost status does not raise willingness to pay, it correlates with charging less. Combined with Pascal’s Quasi-Poisson result (Superhosts get 2.6× more bookings), the picture is consistent: Superhosts compete on price and earn through volume, not through a price premium. The practical takeaway for hosts aiming for the badge is to price competitively and let occupancy do the work.

9.6 Limitations

A few things to keep in mind:

  • Median split is rough. A listing one franc above the median counts the same as one 500 francs above. The LM and GAM handle this much better.
  • No location. We left location out to keep the focus on host variables, but in Vaud, location is a major price driver. Some of what we attribute to room type or capacity is probably picking up location indirectly.
  • Snapshot only. No seasonality, no weekday effects, no events.
  • Correlation, not causation. Superhosts charge less on average but we can’t say the badge causes it. It’s just as likely that hosts who already price competitively end up qualifying for the badge.
  • The cutoff moves with the market. The median is just our sample’s median. If the market shifts, so does it.

10 GAM (DAVIDE)

10.1 Theory

Why GAM for Airbnb Pricing? A standard linear model assumes that every predictor moves the price at a constant rate. For Airbnb data that’s almost never true. A Generalized Additive Model (GAM) replaces the fixed coefficients with smooth functions that the model learns from the data itself. Each predictor gets its own curve instead of a single slope, which lets the model bend where the data actually bends.

The result sits between a linear regression and a black-box model: more flexible than the LM, more interpretable than a Random Forest or Neural Network. Each smooth term can still be plotted on its own and read like a coefficient.

Three things in our data make a GAM the right fit:

  1. Price effects are non-linear. Going from 2 to 4 guests roughly doubles the price; going from 8 to 10 barely moves it. Review scores show a similar pattern. A drop from 4.0 to 3.5 hurts much more than the difference between 4.8 and 5.0. A linear model averages these curves and gets both ends wrong.
  2. Location is non-linear by nature. Vaud combines lakeshore towns, urban centres, alpine resorts, and rural areas, each with their own price logic. A 2D smoother over longitude and latitude captures this surface without us having to engineer postcode dummies or distance-to-lake variables. This is the main reason we picked GAM over LM.
  3. Interpretability stays. Hosts should be able to see why a price is recommended, not just get a number from a black box. The partial effect plots make every smooth term readable on its own.

10.2 Link to Hypothesis

The GAM speaks mainly to H2 through the spatial spline s(longitude, latitude), while controlling for H1 via host_is_superhost and review_scores_rating. It is our main tool for checking whether location effects on willingness to pay are non-linear and clustered, something a linear model cannot answer.

10.3 Model

Train: 2527  | Test: 632

We model log(price) rather than the raw price. The price distribution is heavily right-skewed (see EDA-Part), and the log transform stabilises variance and brings the residuals close to normal. Coefficients on the log scale can be read multiplicatively: a coefficient of β corresponds to roughly a \((exp(β) − 1) × 100%\) price change. We use method = “REML” instead of the default. REML is less prone to undersmoothing and gives more reliable confidence intervals


Family: gaussian 
Link function: identity 

Formula:
log(price) ~ s(accommodates) + s(review_scores_rating) + s(log1p(number_of_reviews)) + 
    s(longitude, latitude) + room_type + host_is_superhost + 
    bathrooms

Parametric coefficients:
                      Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)            4.63771    0.02274 203.969  < 2e-16 ***
room_typeHotel room    0.64874    0.15517   4.181 3.01e-05 ***
room_typePrivate room -0.35849    0.02251 -15.926  < 2e-16 ***
room_typeShared room  -0.80666    0.10670  -7.560 5.63e-14 ***
host_is_superhostTRUE -0.01079    0.01840  -0.587    0.557    
bathrooms              0.22000    0.01512  14.554  < 2e-16 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Approximate significance of smooth terms:
                               edf Ref.df       F  p-value    
s(accommodates)              4.577  5.567 117.850  < 2e-16 ***
s(review_scores_rating)      2.999  3.752   6.096 0.000105 ***
s(log1p(number_of_reviews))  2.634  3.290  40.071  < 2e-16 ***
s(longitude,latitude)       24.933 27.971  12.383  < 2e-16 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

R-sq.(adj) =  0.658   Deviance explained = 66.4%
-REML = 1184.9  Scale est. = 0.14127   n = 2527

10.4 Visualisation

The partial effect plots are the main reason to choose a GAM in the first place. Each smooth term is shown on its own, with the shaded area representing the 95% confidence band.

Smooth Terms: Estimated Degrees of Freedom (EDF)
edf Ref.df F p-value
s(accommodates) 4.577 5.567 117.850 0
s(review_scores_rating) 2.999 3.752 6.096 0
s(log1p(number_of_reviews)) 2.634 3.290 40.071 0
s(longitude,latitude) 24.933 27.971 12.383 0 
R-squared:          0.658 
Deviance explained: 0.664

To make the comparison fair, we fit a linear model on the exact same data, target, and predictors. The only difference is the absence of smooth terms.

LM  R²: 0.607 
GAM R²: 0.658

10.4.1 Traffic Light Map

The GAM predicts an expected price for every listing. Comparing actual to expected gives hosts a quick verdict — underpriced, fair, or overpriced. We set the threshold at ±15%, anchored in the residual standard deviation of the GAM on the log scale (exp(σ) − 1 ≈ 15%). Listings outside this band deviate by more than one standard deviation from the model’s expected price. The threshold is data-driven, but it remains a choice. A stricter or looser band would shift the proportions accordingly. This is one of the limitations we discuss below.


Train:

 Fair Price  Overpriced Underpriced 
  0.3276613   0.2588049   0.4135338 

Test:

 Fair Price  Overpriced Underpriced 
  0.3306962   0.2484177   0.4208861

10.4.2 Interactive Map of Vaud

The map below plots every listing in the dataset, coloured by its traffic-light verdict. Each point shows the actual price, the GAM-recommended price, and the percentage deviation when clicked.

10.5 Interpretation GAM

The GAM explains 66.4% of the deviance (adjusted R² = 0.658) on 2,527 training listings. That’s a clear improvement over the LM benchmark (R² = 0.607) on the same data, and the AIC drops from 2,599 to 2,279. A substantial gain that confirms the smooth terms earn their additional degrees of freedom.

Parametric terms Room type is the strongest categorical driver. Compared to entire homes, hotel rooms charge roughly 1.9× more, private rooms are about 30% cheaper, and shared rooms drop another step to around 55% below entire homes. The hotel-room result rests on a small number of listings, so we shouldn’t over-read it. Bathrooms show a clean linear effect. Each additional bathroom adds roughly 25% to the price. Besides room type and capacity, this is the strongest structural driver in the model. Superhost status is the most telling result. After controlling for everything else, the coefficient is essentially zero and not significant. The badge has no measurable effect on the nightly price. This lines up with the GLM Binomial and the Neural Network. Three very different models converge on the same answer.

Smooth terms All four smooth terms are highly significant (p < 0.001) and every EDF is well above 1, which means a linear approximation would have been wrong for each of them.

  • s(accommodates) (EDF = 4.58): the curve rises steeply from 1 to about 6 guests and then flattens. Doubling capacity from 8 to 16 adds far less than doubling from 2 to 4, which is exactly the diminishing-returns pattern we expected.
  • s(review_scores_rating) (EDF = 3.00): the effect is real but modest. The curve trends slightly upward across the range, with a wide confidence band — ratings matter, but they’re not a major price lever.
  • s(log1p(number_of_reviews)) (EDF = 2.63): a slight downward trend. Listings with more reviews tend to be priced lower — consistent with the volume-over-margin pattern we keep seeing.
  • s(longitude, latitude) (EDF = 24.93): by far the most flexible term in the model. The 2D surface shows clear high-price clusters along the Lake Geneva shore and around Lausanne, with sharper drops in rural and alpine areas. This single term is the strongest empirical support for H2 — location is a major, non-linear driver of willingness to pay.

Traffic-light distribution The traffic-light split is consistent across train and test (Fair ≈ 33%, Underpriced ≈ 41%, Overpriced ≈ 25%), so the model generalises well. The slight overweight of “Underpriced” listings is interesting: it suggests that a notable share of hosts are leaving money on the table compared to what the model considers fair given their listing’s features and location.

Take-aways for the pricing tool: 1. Structural features come first. Room type, bathrooms, and capacity together account for most of the explainable price variation that hosts can directly influence. The pricing interface should highlight these. 2. Location effects are real but non-linear. Coordinates as a continuous input clearly outperform postcode dummies — the spatial spline alone justifies the GAM approach for a regional tool. 3. The Superhost badge is an occupancy strategy, not a pricing one. Hosts aiming for the badge should price competitively and let bookings build up.

10.6 Limitations

The GAM is the strongest model in this study, but a few things are worth keeping in mind: Snapshot only. The model is trained on a single point in time. No seasonality, no weekday effects, no Montreux Jazz Festival, no ski season. Recommendations represent an average market price, not a dynamic one.

Listed price isn’t willingness to pay. We use the listed price as a proxy, but it’s what hosts charge, not what guests would actually pay. Listings that sit unbooked at their current price are still in the training data, which probably tilts the model towards slightly optimistic price levels.

±15% is a chosen threshold. It’s anchored in the residual SD, which makes it defensible, but it’s still a choice. Using prediction intervals from the GAM directly, or empirical residual quantiles, would be a more rigorous alternative.

Missing predictors. Amenity counts (pool, parking, Wi-Fi quality), photo quality, view, distance to public transport, noise. None of this is in the dataset. Some of the unexplained variance lives there rather than in the model’s flexibility.

Spatial spline describes, doesn’t explain. It fits the price surface very well but doesn’t tell us why certain locations are expensive. For a pricing tool that’s enough; for causal claims (does proximity to the lake cause higher prices, or just correlate with better properties?) it isn’t.

11 SVM (TIMON)

11.1 Theory

A Support Vector Machine separates classes by drawing the widest possible margin between them. We use a radial kernel. This allows us to define the hyperplane more precisely. As a result, with SVM we do not obtain a coefficient that can be interpreted, but rather a model that enables us to predict which group an observation will fall into. In the following model, we apply exactly that. We look at the traffic light groups defined in the GAM and try to predict, using the available variables, which group a location will fall into. By splitting the data into a training and test dataset, we enable ourselves to test the model, with the drawback that we lose a portion of the training data.

With the SVM, we are not testing any of the hypotheses, but rather the second research question: to what extent can we predict whether the prices of a rental property are justified? The GAM generates a fair market price for each rental property. The SVM, on the other hand, examines whether the current rental prices are justified.

11.2 Model

SVM Confusion Matrix
Fair Price Overpriced Underpriced
Fair Price 113 43 42
Overpriced 28 105 5
Underpriced 68 9 219
Information:
SVM Test Accuracy: 69.15%
Training set size: 2527
Test set size: 632

11.3 Interpretion SVM

The tuned SVM with an RBF kernel reaches 69.15% accuracy on the test set. This means the model clearly outperforms chance, which would be 33% for a three-class model. The main issues arise when classifying Fair Price. This makes sense, however, since this is the only class that is bounded both above and below and therefore does not exhibit the significant deviations that can be accounted for in the other two categories. For a company like Airbnb, the accuracy is likely too low to make predictions. However, the model shows that estimation within such classes is fundamentally possible. The second Research Question is therefore not easy to answer. We are able to predict wether a price for a location is justified, the accuracy is just not good enough for a company as Airbnb.

11.4 Limitations

There are a few clear limitations to the model that can be identified right away. The accuracy is too low for a company like Airbnb to provide pricing recommendations to hosts, especially since the category most severely affected is the Fair Price category. This leads, in particular, to hosts being incorrectly advised to adjust their prices. Once again, the main cause of the inaccuracy in this model is the location data. We suspect that at the very least, the distance to the nearest city center would have a significant impact on the accuracy of the estimates.

12 ANN (PASCAL)

12.1 Theory

DAVIDE Vorschlag: An Artificial Neural Network learns flexible, non-linear relationships by chaining together layers of weighted nodes. Unlike the LM or GAM, it does not produce readable coefficients — it learns a mapping from inputs to output and treats the rest as internal weights. That makes it powerful but opaque. We use the ANN here as a flexibility check: if the much more interpretable GAM already captures most of the signal, the ANN should not gain much from being more flexible. If it does, we are leaving structure on the table.

12.2 Link to Hypothesis

We use the ANN to revisit H1 from a different angle. The Naive ANN tests whether host-centered variables alone (review score, review count, Superhost status, capacity) can predict price. The Expanded ANN adds room and property type as controls. Comparing the two shows whether any apparent Superhost effect survives once we compare like with like — a stronger test than just plotting raw medians.

12.3 Model

[1] "Predicted Prices (Naive Model): Regular Host vs. Superhost"
         [,1]
[1,] 160.1493
[2,] 131.6514
[1] "Predicted Prices (Expanded Model): Regular Host vs. Superhost"
         [,1]
[1,] 493.6510
[2,] 493.3796

12.4 Interpretation ANN

Our initial Neural Network predicted price using only review metrics, listing capacity, and Superhost status. Surprisingly, it priced Superhosts lower (132 CHF) than regular hosts (160 CHF). This was a clear case of omitted variable bias: Superhosts often use competitive pricing to maintain the high occupancy required for the badge, while expensive luxury rentals are usually managed by agencies that don’t hold Superhost status.

We corrected this in a second model by adding room and property types, using One-Hot Encoding to convert these categories into numerical inputs. Once we controlled for these structural factors to compare similar listings, the price gap practically vanished (482.64 CHF for Superhosts vs. 483.29 CHF for regular hosts). This refutes our initial hypothesis: when comparing apples to apples, Superhost status alone does not significantly increase a guest’s willingness to pay.

12.5 Business Case

The nn_expanded model serves as the analytical core of our pricing decision-support system by accurately predicting a listing’s market value based on combined structural and host-centric features. By comparing this to the nn_naive model, we successfully refute our central hypothesis, demonstrating that achieving host_is_superhost status does not command a higher nightly price from guests. Instead, the data reveals that Superhosts often achieve their high status through competitive pricing strategies designed to maximize occupancy rather than nightly margins. Ultimately, a guest’s willingness to pay is dictated almost entirely by physical listing attributes like accommodates, room_type, and property_type. Therefore, we advise hosts to anchor their pricing strictly to their property’s structural features, leveraging their Superhost badge purely as a marketing tool to capture market share and drive booking volume.

12.6 Limitations

A primary limitation of the nn_expanded model is the inherent “black box” nature of Artificial Neural Networks, which drastically reduces the interpretability of individual price drivers compared to transparent linear models. Because neural networks require mathematical normalization, calculating the exact monetary impact of isolated variables like review_scores_rating is complex and requires continuous reverse-scaling. Furthermore, while we corrected for some omitted variable bias by using One-Hot Encoding for property categories, the model still lacks precise geospatial data, meaning the massive price impact of specific, highly desirable neighborhoods remains uncaptured. Testing our hypothesis also relied on creating a mathematically “average” hypothetical listing, which may not perfectly reflect the nuanced reality of distinct luxury or budget segments in the Canton of Vaud. Finally, applying the na.omit() function removed listings with incomplete profiles, which could introduce bias if those missing data points systematically represented a specific market segment, such as poorly managed properties.

13 Synthesis, Conclusion & Recommendations

13.1 What the Models Agree On

Three findings hold across very different model architectures:

The Superhost badge does not raise the nightly price. Four models with completely different mechanics — the linear regression, the logistic regression (GLM Binomial), the spline-based GAM, and the neural network — all return the same answer: after controlling for listing size, room type and other structural features, Superhost status has no measurable positive effect on price. Some models even show a small negative effect. This is the most robust single finding in this study.

Structural features are the real price drivers. Capacity (accommodates), room type, and the number of bathrooms account for most of the explainable variation in price across all models that include them. The GAM shows that an additional bathroom adds roughly 25% to the price; a private room sells for about 30% less than an entire home, all else equal. Reviews and host attributes matter, but their effect is small compared to the physical features a host can describe in a listing.

Location matters, and matters non-linearly. Lake Geneva and Lausanne sit at the top of the price surface; rural and alpine areas drop off sharply. The GAM’s 2D spatial spline captures this with an effective degree of freedom around 25 — far too curved to approximate with linear coordinates or postcode dummies. Any tool that ignores location will systematically misprice listings outside the canton’s average baseline.

13.2 What the Different Lenses Add

The price models and the occupancy model tell complementary halves of the Superhost story:

Lens Finding
LM, GLM Binomial, GAM, ANN Superhosts charge no more (and slightly less) than regular hosts
GLM Poisson Superhosts get 2.6× more bookings than regular hosts

Read together, these results sketch out the Superhost strategy: price competitively, earn through volume. A host who chases the badge by raising prices is doing it backwards. The badge comes from booking volume and consistent review quality — both of which are easier to achieve at a competitive price point.

A second contrast worth highlighting is the Naive ANN versus the Expanded ANN. The naive version, trained only on host variables, predicts a lower price for Superhosts (132 vs 160 CHF). Once room and property type are added, the gap collapses to less than a franc. This isn’t really a disagreement between models — it’s a textbook case of omitted variable bias, and a useful warning against drawing conclusions from raw price comparisons.

13.3 Recommendations for Hosts

For hosts setting or revising their nightly price:

  • Anchor on structure, not status. What a guest actually pays for is the listing itself: room type, capacity, bathrooms, location. The Superhost badge helps with visibility and booking volume, not with the per-night rate.
  • Use the traffic-light tool as a sanity check, not a target. The GAM-based recommendation gives a fair-market range based on what comparable listings achieve. A listing flagged “Underpriced” is leaving money on the table; one flagged “Overpriced” risks sitting empty.
  • If you’re building toward Superhost status, compete on price. The 2.6× occupancy boost comes from satisfied guests and a full calendar. Premium pricing makes both harder. Start with competitive pricing, accumulate reviews, then raise prices selectively once the badge is secure.
  • Review scores matter less than you think for price. Across the pricing models the reward for higher ratings is modest. Reviews matter, but their role is to drive bookings — and through them, the badge — rather than to justify a higher nightly rate.

13.4 Recommendations for Airbnb

For the platform itself, the same data points in three directions:

  • A pricing assistant should use the GAM, not a linear formula. Linear or postcode-based tools will systematically underprice lakeshore listings and overprice rural ones. The 2D spatial spline is the most defensible way to capture local dynamics with the data Airbnb already collects.
  • Coach the “Underpriced” segment. Roughly 41% of listings in the dataset sit below the GAM’s fair-price band. These hosts are the easiest group to help: a recommendation alone could shift their pricing toward market value, lifting both host revenue and platform commission.
  • Add operationalised location features. Raw coordinates work well for a smoother like the GAM but limit simpler tools. Distance to the nearest lake, city centre, ski lift, and public transport stop would let classifiers like the SVM catch up to the GAM’s accuracy — and these features are trivial to compute centrally and serve to every model on the platform.

14 Overall Limitations

Five limitations apply to this entire study, not just to individual models:

Snapshot, no time component. All models are trained on a single point in time. Seasonal pricing, weekday effects, and event-driven demand spikes (Montreux Jazz Festival, ski season, weekends) are nowhere in the data. The recommendations represent an average market price, not a dynamic one. A real production pricing tool would need a time dimension we don’t have.

Listed price as a proxy for willingness to pay. We treat what hosts charge as a proxy for what guests would pay. This holds on average across a large sample, but it breaks down for listings that sit unbooked at their current price — those listings still shape the model and probably tilt it slightly toward optimistic price levels. A true willingness-to-pay study would need booking data, completed transactions, and ideally A/B tests of competing prices.

Missing predictors. Photo quality, amenity counts, listing description sentiment, view from the property, noise levels, distance to public transport — none of this is in the dataset. Some of the unexplained variance (around a third for the GAM) lives in these omitted variables rather than in any model’s limitation.

Reverse causality. Several relationships in our data probably flow in both directions. More bookings produce more reviews, which make Superhost status easier to maintain, which probably increases visibility, which leads to more bookings. None of our models can untangle which effect causes which. For descriptive purposes that’s fine, but the recommendations should be read as “what works on average,” not “what works because of X.”

Vaud-specific. The dataset covers one Swiss canton at one point in time. The price levels, location dynamics (lakeshore premium, alpine resorts), and host behaviours are specific to this market. The methodology transfers; the exact coefficients do not. Applying the same models to Geneva or Bern would require refitting on local data.

15 Use of GenAI

Throughout this machine learning project, we leveraged various Generative AI tools specifically ChatGPT, Gemini, and Claude to support our analytical workflow. A primary use case was troubleshooting complex issues that extended beyond our existing knowledge base. When encountering technical bottlenecks, we used these models to break down and explain the underlying concepts, enabling us to systematically find the right solutions. Furthermore, we utilized GenAI as a critical review mechanism for our modeling approach. We consulted the AI to verify whether we had made the appropriate theoretical assumptions, such as selecting the correct data distributions for our models. It also served as a valuable sounding board to double-check our final analytical interpretations, ensuring our conclusions were statistically sound. Finally, these tools provided essential linguistic support. We actively used GenAI to translate our documentation and findings into professional English, ensuring a high-quality final report.

16 Appendix
